austin-tx.jobMarketSummary: Austin's technology sector expanded by approximately 23% recently, according to local economic reports. Major employers including Tesla (headquarters), Oracle (cloud campus), Indeed, AMD, and Apple have collectively added an estimated 15,000 jobs. The Bureau of Labor Statistics reports the Austin metro area unemployment rate well below the national average. Software engineers typically earn 15-20% above the national median, with strong demand in cloud computing, AI/ML, and semiconductor design.
austin-tx.housingTrends: According to Zillow Observed Rent Index (ZORI) and local MLS snapshots, median rent in Austin reached approximately $2,100 for a one-bedroom apartment. Numbeo cost-of-living data indicate Austin's housing costs are roughly 30% lower than San Francisco but 45% higher than Dallas. The market remains competitive, with average days on market at 25 days. Property taxes in Travis County average 2.26% of assessed value, among the highest in Texas despite no state income tax.
austin-tx.industryFocus: Technology dominates Austin's economy, comprising an estimated 28% of the metro GDP according to the Greater Austin Chamber. Semiconductor manufacturing has surged with Samsung and Texas Instruments expansions. The healthcare sector, led by Ascension Seton and St. David's, employs approximately 85,000 workers. Clean energy and electric vehicle manufacturing represent the fastest-growing segments, with Tesla's Gigafactory employing over 20,000.
austin-tx.qualityOfLife: Austin consistently ranks in the top 5 US cities for work-life balance, per major financial publications. The city offers 300+ days of sunshine annually and extensive outdoor recreation. However, traffic congestion has worsened, with average commute times reaching 28 minutes. The cultural scene includes live music venues, food trucks, and tech meetups. Cost of living is approximately 15% above the national average but offers no state income tax benefits.

dallas-tx.jobMarketSummary: Dallas-Fort Worth's diverse economy added approximately 95,000 jobs recently, according to the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas. Major employers include American Airlines, AT&T, Texas Instruments, and Charles Schwab. The finance and insurance sector employs over 200,000 workers, with JPMorgan Chase and Goldman Sachs expanding operations. Corporate relocations from California and New York continue.
dallas-tx.housingTrends: According to Numbeo cost-of-living data, Dallas median rent at approximately $1,650 for a one-bedroom apartment, roughly 21% lower than Austin. According to the Dallas Morning News, home prices increased 6% year-over-year. The market favors buyers compared to the past few years, with inventory up. Collin and Denton counties show the strongest growth. Property taxes average 2.18% of assessed value.
dallas-tx.industryFocus: Financial services anchor Dallas's economy, representing approximately 22% of metro GDP per the Dallas Regional Chamber. The telecommunications sector, led by AT&T and Ericsson, employs over 45,000 workers. Healthcare is rapidly expanding. The logistics sector benefits from DFW Airport, the world's second-busiest by aircraft movements. Defense contractors like Lockheed Martin and Raytheon maintain significant operations.
dallas-tx.qualityOfLife: Dallas offers a lower cost of living compared to coastal tech hubs, approximately 8% below the national average according to economic research estimates. The metro area features diverse neighborhoods, from urban Uptown to suburban Plano. Average commute times are 27 minutes. The city experiences hot summers but mild winters. Cultural amenities include the Dallas Arts District, professional sports teams, and a growing food scene.
